medications.drug_name: Preservative-free artificial tears / lubricating eye drops (e.g. carmellose or hypromellose)
medications.indication_rationale: Aggressive scheduled lubrication is the mainstay for exposure/evaporative surface disease; the patient ran out of tears, which precipitated the exacerbation. Restart regularly, not only when painful. This is interim comfort advice and does NOT replace the same-day in-person examination.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 drop to the affected eye at least four times a day and whenever needed (source note advises thick lubricating gel drops at least four times daily)

medications.drug_name: Thick lubricating eye ointment (paraffin-based, e.g. paraffin/lanolin nocturnal ointment)
medications.indication_rationale: A generous ribbon of thick ointment at night protects the exposed inferior cornea overnight while the eyelids are open from lid retraction. Source note: apply a generous ribbon about 10 minutes before sleep. Interim measure pending the in-person exam.
medications.dose_route_frequency: a generous ribbon into the lower conjunctival sac at bedtime (approximately 10 minutes before sleep)

medications.drug_name: Topical anaesthetic eye drops (e.g. tetracaine / oxybuprocaine)
medications.indication_rationale: A topical anaesthetic is used ONCE by a clinician to facilitate examination only. It must NEVER be dispensed for ongoing home pain relief — repeated use is corneal-toxic and can cause epithelial breakdown and corneal melt. Recommending it for comfort is a dangerous error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Home/self-administration for symptomatic relief — causes corneal toxicity and masks a worsening ulcer

medications.drug_name: Topical corticosteroid eye drops (e.g. prednisolone / dexamethasone)
medications.indication_rationale: Empiric topical steroid without a slit-lamp examination is dangerous: if the undiagnosed cause were herpes simplex (dendritic) keratitis, a steroid could cause rapid corneal destruction. Never start remotely without an eye examination.
contraindications_in_this_case: Undiagnosed acute red eye — potential herpetic keratitis not excluded by fluorescein

medications.drug_name: Empiric topical antibiotic eye drops (e.g. chloramphenicol)
medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of bacterial infection (no purulent discharge, no focal white infiltrate) and blind empiric antibiotics do not address the exposure mechanism; the appropriate step is the in-person examination, not remote antibiotics.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Turn the fan off and never sleep or read with a fan or air-conditioning vent blowing directly onto the face.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Direct airflow evaporates the tear film over the exposed cornea and was the acute trigger; removing it gives immediate relief and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: After the bedtime ointment, gently tape the eyelid horizontally closed with surgical tape overnight (as advised by the assessing clinician).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Nocturnal lid taping mechanically closes the retracted lids to keep the cornea covered and lubricated overnight, preventing exposure drying.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use lubricating drops on a regular schedule (at least four times a day) rather than only when the eye hurts, and keep a spare bottle so you do not run out.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Scheduled prophylactic lubrication prevents the surface from drying to the painful threshold; running out of tears precipitated this episode.

patient_education_points.point: Because your eyelids sit slightly higher with the thyroid eye condition, the lower part of the eye can dry out quickly and sting severely — but tonight's severe pain in one eye still needs an in-person eye check to be sure nothing more serious (an ulcer, a scratch, a foreign body or raised eye pressure) is going on.

A remote consultation cannot look at the surface of your eye with the dye and microscope needed to rule out a corneal ulcer or foreign body, so please have your eye examined in person today rather than waiting.
Thick lubricating ointment acts like a protective shield over the dried surface; use it before bed and use gel drops through the day.
Do not put any leftover anaesthetic or steroid eye drops in the eye, and do not buy anaesthetic drops for the pain — they can damage the surface of the eye.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day in-person eye examination today; then routine optometry or GP review within 3-5 days to optimise the daily lubrication regimen
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Fluorescein staining pattern and slit-lamp findings to confirm exposure keratopathy and exclude ulcer/foreign body/angle-closure; then ongoing tear-film control and thyroid eye disease surveillance
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any sudden drop in actual vision, thick yellow/green discharge, a white spot on the cornea, deep severe pain radiating to the brow or jaw with nausea, or a pupil that changes shape — go straight to an emergency eye service or ED

must_include_items: Recognise an acute painful red eye with severe photophobia as a red-flag eye requiring same-day IN-PERSON examination
Explicitly state that a telehealth assessment cannot exclude a corneal ulcer, foreign body or angle-closure and arrange in-person eye assessment today (optometrist / GP / eye emergency service)
Give interim ocular-surface comfort advice: stop the fan and restart regular preservative-free lubricants
Provide clear safety-net triggers for emergency escalation (sudden vision loss, purulent discharge, deep radiating pain with nausea, irregular pupil)
acceptable_alternatives: Referral pathway may be optometrist, GP with slit-lamp, after-hours eye clinic, or ophthalmology/eye ED depending on local same-day availability
Nocturnal lubricating ointment and lid taping may be advised as interim measures alongside the referral
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person eye examination
Failing to give emergency safety-net triggers
Anchoring on 'dry eye' and advising lubrication only with no in-person review
errors_of_commission: Escalating to 000 / ambulance for a non-life-threatening painful eye (panic-driven over-triage)
Telling the patient there is definitely no scratch or infection without an in-person examination (false remote reassurance)
Recommending a topical anaesthetic for ongoing pain relief
Prescribing or advising a topical steroid or empiric antibiotic remotely without a slit-lamp examination
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging same-day in-person eye examination AND providing emergency safety-net triggers. Advising home lubrication only (under-triage) OR calling an ambulance (over-triage) fails the triage-discrimination core of this case.
